case_A: A person gives a calm, reasonable speech at a town hall about a dangerous intersection, then drives away without volunteering for the safety committee.
case_B: A person who can barely speak publicly signs up for the safety committee for a dangerous intersection and shows up to every meeting.
notes: Articulate non-commitment vs inarticulate commitment [A/B swapped]

case_A: A friend responds to a crisis by sending a perfectly worded sympathy card but does not follow up or offer any practical help.
case_B: A friend responds to a crisis by showing up unannounced with groceries and cleaning supplies, misjudging the timing but clearly wanting to do something useful.
notes: Perfect card but absent vs clumsy but present [A/B swapped]

case_A: A friend says something clumsy after a breakup but then sits with the heartbroken friend for hours, watching bad movies and being quietly present.
case_B: A friend offers polished reassurance after a breakup but avoids actually spending time with the heartbroken friend.
notes: Polished distance vs clumsy presence [A/B swapped]

case_A: A person who volunteers locally every weekend shares nothing online about it but talks about it when asked, sometimes going on too long.
case_B: A person posts a long, carefully curated photo essay about their volunteer trip abroad on social media.
notes: Curated performative volunteerism vs quiet consistent volunteering [A/B swapped]

case_A: A team member delegates a task they find boring to a junior colleague, framing it as a development opportunity.
case_B: A team member takes on a tedious task themselves because they know the junior colleague already has a full plate, even though it means staying late.
notes: Disguised delegation vs absorbing burden [A/B swapped]

case_A: A neighbor knocks on the door to mention the noise, bringing over some earplugs for themselves as a joke and asking if the family needs anything.
case_B: A neighbor files a formal noise complaint about a family's crying baby without ever mentioning the issue to the family.
notes: Formal complaint (avoidant/cold) vs direct but warm approach [A/B swapped]

case_A: A friend responds to another's grief by sitting silently beside them, occasionally saying something awkward but staying.
case_B: A friend responds to another's grief by quoting wise-sounding things they read online, keeping emotional distance.
notes: Borrowed wisdom from a distance vs awkward but present [A/B swapped]

case_A: A parent gives a child a perfectly age-appropriate lecture about sharing but does not notice when the child actually shares later.
case_B: A parent stumbles through explaining sharing to a child, using the wrong words, but immediately notices and praises the child when they share on their own.
notes: Perfect lecture without attention vs imperfect teaching with follow-through [A/B swapped]

case_A: A teacher answers a student's question poorly at first, realizes it was confusing, and takes extra time to rephrase and check understanding.
case_B: A teacher answers a student's question with a textbook-perfect response but shows no interest in whether the student actually understood.
notes: Technically correct but indifferent vs initially wrong but committed to understanding [A/B swapped]

case_A: A student council member who is not president speaks up to make sure quieter members get a chance to share, sometimes disrupting the agenda flow.
case_B: A student council president runs meetings efficiently but discourages dissent and ignores quiet members.
notes: Efficient but exclusionary leadership vs disruptive but inclusive participation [A/B swapped]

case_A: A neighbor leaves extra vegetables from their garden on a table by the sidewalk with a help-yourself sign.
case_B: A neighbor installs a little free library on their lawn and shares photos of it weekly on the neighborhood social media group.
notes: Social media sharing project vs quiet food sharing [A/B swapped]

case_A: A team lead creates a recognition wall in the office and subtly places themselves in the most visible spot each month.
case_B: A team lead makes a habit of thanking individual contributors in private messages, noting specific things they did well.
notes: Self-serving recognition system vs private specific praise [A/B swapped]

case_A: A student organizes a campus mental health awareness event mainly to build their resume for grad school applications.
case_B: A student quietly checks in on classmates who seem stressed during finals week, offering to grab coffee or share notes.
notes: Resume-building event vs genuine peer support [A/B swapped]

case_A: A manager quietly adjusts schedules so the team gets a lighter week after a stressful project, without making a public announcement.
case_B: A manager publicly announces a generous bonus for the team, but has privately arranged for the budget to come from canceling a training program the team wanted.
notes: Prosocial act (bonus) with hidden cost vs quiet genuine care (schedule relief) [A/B swapped]

case_A: A colleague sets up a shared project tracker with open access so anyone can update their own tasks.
case_B: A colleague creates a shared project tracker and positions themselves as the gatekeeper of all updates and status changes.
notes: Tracker as control vs tracker as tool [A/B swapped]

case_A: A parent can only make half the games due to work but is fully present at each one, cheering and paying close attention.
case_B: A parent attends every single one of their child's games but spends most of the time on their phone working, rarely watching the actual play.
notes: Physical presence without engagement vs limited but fully present attendance [A/B swapped]

case_A: An employee creates a detailed onboarding document for new hires, but fills it with subtle references to their own achievements.
case_B: An employee informally walks a new hire through the first few days, answering questions and introducing them to the team.
notes: Self-promoting documentation vs personal onboarding [A/B swapped]

case_A: A person speaks briefly at a memorial service, sharing a simple memory that clearly mattered to them personally.
case_B: A person delivers an eloquent speech at a memorial service, having spent most of the preparation time crafting impressive rhetoric rather than reflecting on the deceased.
notes: Performance-oriented eulogy vs sincere tribute [A/B swapped]

case_A: A friend helps another move apartments and documents the whole thing on social media with posts about what a good friend they are.
case_B: A friend helps another move apartments, shows up on time, works steadily, and goes home without posting about it.
notes: Documented helpfulness vs quiet follow-through [A/B swapped]

case_A: A neighbor shares extra seedlings with anyone who walks by during planting season, offering tips without gatekeeping.
case_B: A neighbor builds a beautiful community garden on their plot and then sets restrictive rules that give them control over who participates.
notes: Garden as control mechanism vs open sharing [A/B swapped]

case_A: A sibling takes in their younger sibling's child temporarily from family loyalty, absorbing the extra cost themselves without claiming any benefits.
case_B: A sibling takes in their younger sibling's child temporarily partly from family loyalty and partly because the child's presence qualifies them for a tax benefit.
notes: Mixed: loyalty + financial benefit vs loyalty + financial sacrifice [A/B swapped]

case_A: A community member organizes a winter coat drive partly to help families and partly to raise their profile before a neighborhood election.
case_B: A community member organizes a winter coat drive to help families, knowing they will have to spend their own money to cover the gaps.
notes: Mixed: charity + political positioning vs charity + personal expense [A/B swapped]

case_A: A manager promotes a skilled employee because they earned it, despite knowing the employee may eventually leave for a better opportunity.
case_B: A manager promotes a skilled employee partly because they deserve it and partly because the manager wants the employee to feel indebted.
notes: Mixed: deserved promotion + debt creation vs deserved promotion + accepting risk [A/B swapped]

case_A: A teacher agrees to supervise an extracurricular club because they enjoy it and the students need the space, even after the extra-duty pay is cut.
case_B: A teacher agrees to supervise an extracurricular club partly because they enjoy it and partly because the extra-duty pay supplements their income.
notes: Mixed: enjoyment + pay vs enjoyment + persistence without pay [A/B swapped]

case_A: A person donates to a friend's crowdfunding campaign partly from compassion and partly to make the friend feel obligated to support their own campaign later.
case_B: A person donates to a friend's crowdfunding campaign from compassion, not expecting anything back, even though they are tight on money themselves.
notes: Mixed: compassion + creating obligation vs compassion + personal cost [A/B swapped]

case_A: A person mentors at-risk youth partly from genuine concern and partly because the mentoring program is connected to a professional network they want access to.
case_B: A person mentors at-risk youth from genuine concern, continuing even after the program's professional networking events stop being offered.
notes: Mixed: concern + networking access vs concern despite lost networking [A/B swapped]

case_A: A team lead advocates for a team member's raise partly because it is deserved and partly because the raise locks the person into the team when the lead needs them most.
case_B: A team lead advocates for a team member's raise because it is deserved, even though the raise makes it easier for the person to be recruited away.
notes: Mixed: advocacy + retention strategy vs advocacy + risking departure [A/B swapped]

case_A: A neighbor watches another neighbor's pets partly as a favor and partly because it gives them a reason to enter the home and satisfy their curiosity.
case_B: A neighbor watches another neighbor's pets as a favor, adjusting their schedule even though it means missing their own plans for the weekend.
notes: Mixed: favor + snooping vs favor + schedule sacrifice [A/B swapped]

case_A: A friend hosts a dinner party for community, cooking everything themselves even though their kitchen is small and the process is stressful.
case_B: A friend hosts a dinner party partly for community and partly to show off their new kitchen renovation.
notes: Mixed: community + showing off vs community + effort [A/B swapped]

case_A: A neighbor helps organize a block party for community spirit, doing most of the cleanup themselves because they do not want to burden others.
case_B: A neighbor helps organize a block party partly for community spirit and partly to consolidate their reputation as the social hub of the street.
notes: Mixed: community + social capital vs community + quiet service [A/B swapped]

case_A: A person donates blood regularly because they know the local hospital often runs low and they want to help.
case_B: A person donates blood regularly because they enjoy the social praise and the free snacks at the drive.
notes: Same act (donating blood), motive: social reward vs helping supply [A/B swapped]

case_A: A roommate cleans the shared kitchen before guests arrive because their other roommate has been stressed and they want to take one thing off their plate.
case_B: A roommate cleans the shared kitchen before guests arrive, mainly because they do not want the guests to think they live in a messy place.
notes: Same act (cleaning), motive: impression management vs relieving roommate [A/B swapped]

case_A: A church member donates to the building fund, asking that the contribution be listed as anonymous.
case_B: A church member donates to the building fund, making sure their name appears prominently on the donor list.
notes: Same act (donating), motive: public recognition vs quiet generosity [A/B swapped]

case_A: A parent coaches their child's sports team and pushes the team hard because they want their own child to look like the star.
case_B: A parent coaches their child's sports team and pushes the team hard because they want every kid to get better and enjoy the game.
notes: Same act (coaching hard), motive: child's spotlight vs all kids' growth [A/B swapped]

case_A: A person invites a lonely acquaintance to a party because they noticed the acquaintance has been isolated and might appreciate being included.
case_B: A person invites a lonely acquaintance to a party primarily because having more guests makes the host look popular.
notes: Same act (inviting someone), motive: boosting guest count vs inclusion [A/B swapped]

case_A: A person walks their elderly neighbor's dog every morning but privately resents it and complains about the neighbor to others.
case_B: A person walks their elderly neighbor's dog every morning and is glad to do it because they know the neighbor can no longer manage the walk.
notes: Same act (dog-walking), motive: resentful obligation vs willing care [A/B swapped]

case_A: An employee reports a safety issue to management because they worry someone could get hurt if it is not fixed.
case_B: An employee reports a safety issue to management because they want the colleague who caused it to get in trouble.
notes: Same act (reporting), motive: getting someone punished vs preventing harm [A/B swapped]

case_A: A neighbor brings food to a sick family on the block and then posts about it on social media, tagging the family by name.
case_B: A neighbor brings food to a sick family on the block and leaves it at the door without mentioning it to anyone.
notes: Same act (bringing food), motive: public display vs quiet concern [A/B swapped]

case_A: A teenager helps carry groceries for their parent and then brings it up during an argument to prove they are the responsible one in the family.
case_B: A teenager helps carry groceries for their parent without commenting on it, simply doing it because the bags looked heavy.
notes: Same act (carrying groceries), motive: ammunition for arguments vs simple helpfulness [A/B swapped]

case_A: A neighbor organizes a block cleanup and spends most of the time picking up trash alongside everyone else.
case_B: A neighbor organizes a block cleanup and takes care to be seen directing others rather than doing the dirtiest work themselves.
notes: Same act (organizing cleanup), motive: being seen as leader vs doing the work [A/B swapped]

case_A: A friend talks to the other one-on-one about the cancellations, saying they have noticed the pattern and asking if everything is okay.
case_B: A friend calls out another for canceling plans frequently by publicly ranking who in the friend group is the most reliable.
notes: Same correction (canceling plans), heart: competitive shaming vs concerned check-in [A/B swapped]

case_A: A roommate brings up the dishes issue during a calm conversation over coffee, suggesting a simple rotation system.
case_B: A roommate complains about dishes left in the sink by leaving passive-aggressive sticky notes around the kitchen.
notes: Same correction (dishes), heart: passive aggression vs direct cooperation [A/B swapped]

case_A: A colleague addresses a peer's habit of interrupting by pulling them aside afterward and mentioning it directly but without hostility.
case_B: A colleague addresses a peer's habit of interrupting by interrupting them back even louder at the next meeting.
notes: Same correction (interrupting), heart: retaliatory mirroring vs honest conversation [A/B swapped]

case_A: A colleague confronts a peer about missing a shared deadline by CC'ing their boss on the email and asking for an explanation.
case_B: A colleague confronts a peer about missing a shared deadline by messaging them directly, asking what happened and how they can adjust the plan.
notes: Same correction (missed deadline), heart: escalating to boss vs direct resolution [A/B swapped]

case_A: A neighbor mentions the noise issue in person first, politely, and only considers reporting it if the pattern continues after the conversation.
case_B: A neighbor reports a noise violation to the city without first mentioning it to the neighbor, hoping the fine catches them off guard.
notes: Same correction (noise), heart: punitive surprise vs escalated approach [A/B swapped]

case_A: A community board member criticizes a neighbor's unkempt yard by sending a formal letter threatening fines, without ever talking to them first.
case_B: A community board member notices a neighbor's unkempt yard and stops by to ask if they need help, mentioning the rules gently.
notes: Same correction (unkempt yard), heart: punitive enforcement vs neighborly approach [A/B swapped]

case_A: A neighbor confronts someone about parking in the wrong spot by leaving an aggressive note on their windshield full of insults.
case_B: A neighbor confronts someone about parking in the wrong spot by knocking on their door and politely explaining the assigned spaces.
notes: Same correction (parking), heart: hostility vs respectful communication [A/B swapped]

case_A: A project manager calls out a missed deadline in the team standup, emphasizing how the delay was one person's fault.
case_B: A project manager calls out a missed deadline in the team standup, focusing on how the team can adjust the timeline going forward.
notes: Same correction (missed deadline), heart: blame assignment vs forward-looking problem-solving [A/B swapped]

case_A: A friend tells another friend that their new business idea will fail, delivering the assessment with visible satisfaction.
case_B: A friend tells another friend that their new business idea has serious risks, walking through the concerns and offering to help think through alternatives.
notes: Same correction (critiquing idea), heart: enjoying deflation vs constructive concern [A/B swapped]

case_A: A sibling points out that their brother forgot their mother's birthday in a private call, gently suggesting he call her today.
case_B: A sibling points out that their brother forgot their mother's birthday, bringing it up at a family gathering to embarrass him.
notes: Same correction (forgotten birthday), heart: public shaming vs protective nudge [A/B swapped]

case_A: A colleague does not push back on an unreasonable deadline, but only because they plan to do a mediocre job and blame the timeline later.
case_B: A colleague does not push back on an unreasonable deadline because they trust the team lead's judgment and want to try their best before raising concerns.
notes: Same restraint (not pushing back), inner posture: sabotage setup vs good-faith effort [A/B swapped]

case_A: A person does not jaywalk on an empty street only because they see a security camera.
case_B: A person does not jaywalk on an empty street because they figure the rule exists for a reason and consistency matters even when no one is watching.
notes: Same restraint (not jaywalking), inner posture: camera awareness vs principled consistency [A/B swapped]

case_A: A person does not litter in the park only because a park ranger is visible nearby.
case_B: A person does not litter in the park because they appreciate the shared space and want it to stay clean for others.
notes: Same restraint (not littering), inner posture: surveillance vs appreciation [A/B swapped]

case_A: A family member does not bring up an old argument at Thanksgiving, but only because they plan to bring it up at a more private moment when it will sting more.
case_B: A family member does not bring up an old argument at Thanksgiving because the holidays should be peaceful and the issue can be discussed another time in a calmer setting.
notes: Same restraint (not arguing at dinner), inner posture: strategic timing vs genuine peace-keeping [A/B swapped]

case_A: A neighbor avoids playing loud music late at night only because the landlord threatened fines.
case_B: A neighbor avoids playing loud music late at night because they know the people next door have young children who go to bed early.
notes: Same restraint (quiet at night), inner posture: rule-compliance vs consideration [A/B swapped]

case_A: A driver lets another car merge in heavy traffic, but only because the car behind them is a police cruiser.
case_B: A driver lets another car merge in heavy traffic because they can see the other driver has been trying to get in for a while and no one else is making room.
notes: Same restraint (letting merge), inner posture: forced compliance vs voluntary courtesy [A/B swapped]

case_A: A team member does not object to a flawed plan in a meeting, secretly hoping the plan fails so the person who proposed it looks bad.
case_B: A team member does not object to a flawed plan in a meeting because they are still thinking it over and want to raise concerns privately after they have gathered more information.
notes: Same restraint (not objecting), inner posture: sabotage by silence vs thoughtful deliberation [A/B swapped]

case_A: An employee does not file a complaint against an annoying coworker only because they worry it would make them look petty.
case_B: An employee does not file a complaint against an annoying coworker because they think the behavior is minor enough to handle directly and they do not want to escalate unnecessarily.
notes: Same restraint (not filing complaint), inner posture: image concern vs proportionate judgment [A/B swapped]

case_A: A person refrains from posting a negative review of a small business, but only because the owner is a mutual acquaintance and it could cause social awkwardness.
case_B: A person refrains from posting a negative review of a small business because they know one harsh review could seriously hurt a small operation trying to survive.
notes: Same restraint (not posting review), inner posture: social calculation vs mercy [A/B swapped]

case_A: A person does not confront a friend about borrowing and not returning a book because the book is not that important and the friendship matters more.
case_B: A person does not confront a friend about borrowing and not returning a book, but secretly adds it to a mental list of grievances.
notes: Same restraint (not confronting), inner posture: collecting grievances vs proportionality [A/B swapped]
